title.en: Letting someone else take the visibility
title.es: Dejar que otra persona tome la visibilidad

question.en: Tell me about a time you let someone else take the visibility on a project you contributed heavily to. Why did you make that call, and what did you do instead of being in front?
question.es: Cuéntame sobre una ocasión en la que dejaste que otra persona tomara la visibilidad en un proyecto al que contribuiste en gran medida. ¿Por qué tomaste esa decisión, y qué hiciste en lugar de estar al frente?

must_include: the specific teammate named by name and role — ST stories without a named peer are structurally suspect
what the candidate specifically chose not to claim — the work, the presentation, the credit attribution they gave up, not paraphrased as 'I stepped back'
the reason the handoff was the right call for the team or for that specific person — not a statement that it was the right thing, but the reasoning that drove it
what the candidate did instead — the unglamorous or invisible contribution they made while the named person had the visible role

evidence of how the handoff was received — by the teammate, the audience, or the project outcome — as an observation, not self-validation ('she told me afterward it was the first time she'd presented to a VP')
must_avoid: flat 'we shared credit' framing — sharing credit is not handing it off; this story requires the candidate to have made themselves invisible while someone else was visible
hero narrative in handoff clothing — 'I coached them through it and they did great' still centers the candidate as the enabler; the story should center the teammate and what they achieved
'I also shipped X' appended to the handoff story — individual output conflated with the team-visibility decision undermines the ST signal
describing the handoff as a sacrifice or moral act — the framing should be matter-of-fact: this was what the team needed, not a test of character the candidate passed
